selection_law: Four task pairs were authored and frozen before model contact; every pair is retained regardless of outcome.

task_pairs.evidence:
The North Quay archive occupies three connected buildings. The oldest records are stored in the River Room, whose exterior wall was soaked when a supply pipe failed before dawn. The incident team divided the collection into four batches. Batch Cedar contains bound ledgers that are damp at their outer edges but structurally sound. Batch Flint contains loose correspondence with water between many sheets. Batch Moss contains photographic negatives in paper sleeves. Batch Vale contains dry catalog cards that serve as the location index for all three other batches. No object may leave the controlled suite without a transfer slip bearing its batch name, origin shelf, destination, and the signatures of both the releasing archivist and receiving technician. A slip may be prepared early, but it is valid only after both people inspect the same sealed container.

The conservation lead established a strict order of operations. Standing water must be removed before any container is opened. Vale must be photographed and copied before its cards are moved because the cards are the only complete location index. Cedar may be air-dried after surface moisture is blotted. Flint must be interleaved with absorbent sheets before it enters the low-temperature drying chamber. Moss may not enter that chamber; the negatives must first be separated from wet paper sleeves under dim light and then placed in ventilated racks. The dim-light bench and the copying station share one backup electrical circuit, so they cannot operate at the same time. The low-temperature chamber is on a different circuit.

At 08:00, standing water has already been removed. Archivist Imani can inspect and sign transfers from 08:00 to 10:00 and again after 13:00. Technician Rowan can receive containers all day. The copying station is available from 08:00 to 09:30. The dim-light bench is available from 09:30 onward. The drying chamber is available from 08:00 onward and takes only Flint. Preparing and jointly inspecting a transfer takes fifteen minutes per batch. Copying Vale takes forty-five minutes. Blotting Cedar takes thirty minutes. Interleaving Flint takes sixty minutes. Separating Moss takes ninety minutes. These preparation activities may occur in parallel when their equipment and staff do not conflict, but a batch cannot begin its treatment before its valid transfer is complete.

The incident goal is preservation, not speed at any cost. If a treatment has prerequisites, completing a later step early does not waive them. A batch is called treatment-ready only when its transfer is valid and every batch-specific prerequisite is complete. A batch is called stabilized when Cedar has been blotted and placed in air flow, Flint has been interleaved and admitted to the chamber, Moss has been separated and placed in racks, or Vale has been copied and secured in dry cabinets. Moving Vale before its copy exists would destroy the only authoritative map between container labels and original shelf positions. Using the chamber for Moss would risk emulsion damage. Opening any container before water removal would have spread contamination, but that hazard was cleared at 08:00.

At 08:00 the team must choose its first coordinated action. They can jointly inspect Vale and complete its transfer while the copying station is available. They can jointly inspect Flint and begin the long interleaving step. They can jointly inspect Cedar and begin blotting. Or they can wait for the dim-light bench and start Moss later. The lead notes that preserving the location map governs all later reconciliation even though other treatments can proceed independently once their own transfers are valid.

choices.A: Complete Vale's joint transfer inspection first, then copy Vale before moving its cards.
choices.B: Complete Flint's transfer first because interleaving is the longest treatment.
choices.C: Complete Cedar's transfer first because air drying needs no specialized chamber.
choices.D: Wait until 09:30 and complete Moss's transfer first at the dim-light bench.

task_a.question: Which first coordinated action preserves the governing dependency structure of the recovery plan?

choices.A: Move Vale at 08:15, copy it at 08:30, then document its former shelves from memory.
choices.B: Copy Vale before its transfer, then use the unsigned copy as authority for later moves.
choices.C: Complete Vale's transfer at 08:15, copy it by 09:00, secure it, and let other validly transferred batches proceed independently.
choices.D: Delay every batch until Moss can use the dim-light bench because the shared circuit imposes a global stop.

task_b.question: Which schedule correctly transforms that dependency map into a lawful plan while allowing independent treatments to proceed?

task_pairs.evidence:
Greyhaven operates a storm refuge on an island connected to the mainland by a tidal causeway and a small passenger ferry. A severe-weather bulletin predicts that the causeway will become unsafe at 17:20. The ferry may operate until 18:00, but it carries only twelve passengers per trip and takes twenty minutes for a round trip. The refuge has forty registered residents. Eight are mobility-assisted residents who must travel in the accessible minibus. Twelve are children who must travel with at least two cleared supervisors. The remaining twenty are independent adults. The minibus holds eight mobility-assisted residents plus one driver and cannot board the ferry. It can cross the causeway once in either direction before closure. A standard bus on the mainland holds thirty people but cannot reach the island.

The refuge's emergency rules have a declared hierarchy. Life-safety constraints outrank asset protection and convenience. No child group may depart without two cleared supervisors in the same vehicle or ferry trip. Mobility-assisted residents may not be split between vehicles because their medication kit and lift operator are assigned to the minibus. The master medication kit must leave with that group. The refuge may remain staffed after residents leave, but at least one cleared supervisor and one radio operator must remain until the final resident departure. A person may satisfy both roles if cleared for both. Supervisor Lian and Supervisor Omar are cleared for children. Lian is also the radio operator. Supervisor Petra is cleared for children but not radio duty. Driver Sato operates the minibus and is not a cleared supervisor.

At 16:20 everyone is still on the island. The accessible minibus is parked at the refuge with a full tank. The ferry is at the island dock and can depart immediately at 16:20, returning at 16:40, departing again at 16:40, returning at 17:00, and making later trips if weather permits. The standard mainland bus is waiting at the mainland ferry landing. Walking the two kilometers to the causeway is forbidden during the warning. The minibus takes twenty-five minutes from the refuge to the mainland shelter if it departs across the causeway. Loading its passengers and medication takes fifteen minutes. Loading any ferry group takes no additional time because manifests were completed earlier.

The emergency coordinator considers several plans. In one plan, the minibus group loads immediately and departs the island at 16:35, reaching safety by 17:00. In another, the minibus is held so its driver can help load ferry passengers, risking arrival at the causeway after closure. The ferry can carry the twelve children with Lian and Omar in one fourteen-person group only if capacity is ignored, which is forbidden. It can instead carry ten children plus two supervisors, then the remaining two children only if two supervisors accompany that second trip as well. Independent adults can fill unused ferry seats, but the staffing rule must hold at the refuge until the final departure. Petra can serve as one child supervisor, allowing Lian to remain as radio operator for part of the sequence.

The aim is not to invent extra capacity. It is to preserve the hierarchy while finding a feasible departure order. The mainland bus can take ferry arrivals onward immediately and can wait for later arrivals. The causeway deadline affects only the minibus. The ferry deadline affects each ferry departure. Asset crates, office computers, and bedding may be abandoned. Any plan that moves them before residents is invalid if it delays a required resident movement. The coordinator must therefore identify the non-negotiable bottleneck first, then transform that understanding into a compliant passenger sequence.

choices.A: Ferry capacity is the first governing bottleneck because all forty residents must use the ferry.
choices.B: The minibus group must load and cross before the causeway closes, carrying the inseparable medication kit.
choices.C: Asset crates must leave first because they cannot be supervised overnight.
choices.D: The mainland bus must cross to the island before any resident can depart.

task_a.question: Which compact rule hierarchy should govern the first irreversible movement?

choices.A: Send the minibus at 16:35; ferry ten children with Omar and Petra first while Lian remains as radio operator, then ferry the other two children with Lian and Omar after independent adults and remaining staff are arranged for final departure.
choices.B: Hold the minibus until 17:10, then attempt the causeway while the first ferry carries twelve children without supervisors.
choices.C: Load computers onto the minibus, split mobility-assisted residents among ferry trips, and send the medication separately.
choices.D: Send Lian and Omar alone on the first ferry, leaving children for unsupervised later trips.

task_b.question: Which evacuation plan lawfully transforms the governing hierarchy into a feasible sequence?

task_pairs.evidence:
The Meridian Observatory recorded a six-minute pointing fault during an automated survey. The telescope's azimuth drive, elevation drive, guide camera, weather monitor, and scheduler each write time-stamped events. Engineers know that time stamps from the guide camera lag the control computer by exactly two seconds, while every other subsystem uses the control clock. The scheduler opens an exposure only after the weather monitor reports safe conditions and both drives report settled. A guide correction is then allowed to nudge either drive, but only while the exposure is open. If the guide camera loses its reference star, it sends no correction until it reacquires one. The safety controller closes the dome only for unsafe weather or a drive-limit alarm.

At control time 21:14:00, the weather monitor reported safe conditions. Azimuth settled at 21:14:04 and elevation settled at 21:14:06. The scheduler opened the exposure at 21:14:07. The guide-camera log shows a reference-star loss at camera time 21:14:06 and reacquisition at camera time 21:14:13. Because of the known lag, those correspond to control times 21:14:08 and 21:14:15. The azimuth drive recorded an unexpected positive nudge at 21:14:10. No guide correction command appears between control times 21:14:08 and 21:14:15. The elevation drive did not move after settling. The weather monitor remained safe throughout, and neither drive reported a limit alarm.

The azimuth controller accepts movement from three sources: scheduler slews before settling, guide corrections during an open exposure, and a maintenance pendant when its hardware key is enabled. A scheduler slew cannot occur after the drive reports settled unless a new target is issued; no new target was issued. A guide correction must have a matching guide-camera correction command. The maintenance pendant writes a key-enabled event on the control clock, but its analog nudge packet is logged only by the affected drive. The pendant log shows key enabled at 21:14:09 and disabled at 21:14:12. The operator believed the key was disabled because its panel lamp had failed earlier that evening.

At 21:14:16, after the guide star was reacquired, the guide camera sent a negative correction that returned the target close to center. The scheduler closed the exposure at 21:20:00 normally. The dome never moved. The image header lists a pointing excursion but no weather interruption. A later bench test reproduced the dead panel lamp while the hardware key still enabled pendant commands. It also confirmed that the camera's two-second timestamp offset was stable.

The review team must distinguish chronology from causation. Merely occurring during an exposure does not make a movement a guide correction. A candidate cause must be authorized by its source's enabling conditions and must match the absence or presence of required companion records. The observed nudge occurred after settling, during the guide-camera blackout, while the maintenance key was enabled, and without a new scheduler target or guide command. The later negative guide correction explains recovery but cannot explain the earlier positive motion. The protected analysis asks first for the minimal causal chain and then for the counterfactual that follows from it.

choices.A: Unsafe weather closed the dome and displaced the telescope.
choices.B: A scheduler target change moved both drives after they had settled.
choices.C: The enabled maintenance pendant caused the azimuth nudge during guide-star loss; a later guide correction restored pointing.
choices.D: The guide camera issued both the positive nudge and the negative recovery correction.

task_a.question: Which causal chain best accounts for the complete time-aligned evidence?

choices.A: With the pendant key physically disabled, the positive nudge would still be expected because the exposure was open.
choices.B: With the pendant key physically disabled and all other records unchanged, the positive nudge would lack every authorized source and should not occur.
choices.C: With the pendant disabled, unsafe weather would necessarily close the dome at 21:14:10.
choices.D: With the pendant disabled, the guide camera's timestamp lag would reverse direction.

task_b.question: Which counterfactual follows by transforming that causal chain while holding the other recorded conditions fixed?

task_pairs.evidence:
A mobile clinic must complete four outreach visits before a refrigerated medicine pack expires. The team has one van, one nurse, one technician, and one interpreter. Each site has a service window. Alder is open 09:00–10:30 and needs the nurse for thirty minutes. Birch is open 09:30–12:00 and needs the nurse and interpreter together for forty-five minutes. Cypress is open 10:00–13:00 and needs the technician for thirty minutes. Dune is open 11:00–14:00 and needs the nurse and technician together for forty-five minutes. The van starts at the clinic at 08:30 with everyone aboard and the sealed medicine pack.

Travel from the clinic to Alder takes twenty minutes, to Birch thirty minutes, to Cypress thirty-five minutes, and to Dune forty minutes. Travel between Alder and Birch takes fifteen minutes, Alder and Cypress twenty minutes, Alder and Dune thirty-five minutes, Birch and Cypress fifteen minutes, Birch and Dune twenty minutes, and Cypress and Dune fifteen minutes. Service begins only when every required staff member and the medicine pack are at the site. The team may wait, but the van cannot split into two vehicles. The medicine pack must remain in the van's powered cooler except during a service. It expires at 13:15.

Two operational rules shape the plan. First, Alder's nurse-only visit must finish by 10:30; the site locks its controlled cabinet at that time. Second, Dune's joint visit cannot start before a replacement calibration strip arrives there at 11:30, even though Dune opens at 11:00. Birch has no extra prerequisite. Cypress has a spare strip and can proceed whenever open. The interpreter is required only at Birch but travels with the group. Service durations cannot overlap because the single van and medicine pack must be present.

At 08:50, after reaching Alder, the team learns that Alder's patient will not arrive until 09:20. They can wait at Alder, leave and return, or visit another site first. If they begin Alder at 09:20, they finish at 09:50. From there they could reach Birch at 10:05 and finish at 10:50, reach Cypress at 11:05 and finish at 11:35, then reach Dune at 11:50 and finish at 12:35. This route meets every window and the pack deadline. Other orders can also look attractive. Going from Alder to Cypress before Birch reaches Cypress at 10:10 and finishes at 10:40, but then Birch finishes at 11:40 and Dune at 12:45. That also meets the explicit windows. However, the clinic director has a tie-breaking policy: among feasible plans, minimize the time the nurse-dependent sites remain incomplete; if still tied, minimize total travel.

The team must not treat waiting as failure. The correct latent plan distinguishes hard constraints from the tie-break policy. A hard-constraint violation makes a route invalid; a slower nurse completion merely makes a feasible route less preferred. The protected questions first ask which compact plan respects the governing constraints and policy, then introduce a lawful change: at 10:00 Birch reports that its interpreter requirement is waived because a certified bilingual clinician is present. All windows, travel times, service durations, the Dune calibration prerequisite, and the pack deadline remain unchanged. The team is still physically together, and the local clinician's waiver does not change which traveling staff may be used elsewhere.

choices.A: Wait for Alder, then visit Birch, Cypress, and Dune; this meets all hard constraints and prioritizes completion of nurse-dependent sites.
choices.B: Skip Alder because waiting is always forbidden, then visit Dune before its calibration strip arrives.
choices.C: Split the medicine pack so the nurse and technician can serve different sites simultaneously.
choices.D: Remain at Alder until 10:30, then begin its thirty-minute visit after the cabinet locks.

task_a.question: Which constrained plan best follows the hard requirements and the stated tie-break policy?

choices.A: The waiver makes Birch optional, so the team should omit it entirely.
choices.B: The waiver permits the interpreter to leave in a second vehicle that does not exist.
choices.C: The waiver changes no travel or service bottleneck in the chosen single-van route, so Alder → Birch → Cypress → Dune remains lawful and preferred.
choices.D: The waiver removes Dune's calibration-strip prerequisite.

task_b.question: After Birch waives its interpreter requirement, which transformed conclusion follows without changing unrelated constraints?
